title: Behold the Lamb of God
subtitle: John points to Jesus at the Jordan: "Behold the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world"

stillness: Be still now, and let the noise of the world fall away.
Come down from the desert of your distractions to the riverbank.
Wait in silence for the One who is coming toward you.
gospel: On the next day, after his return from the desert,
the Lord came to the Jordan,
and John saw Jesus coming toward him.
He pointed to him with his finger,
behold him who takes away the sin of the world.
This is he of whom I said:
After me comes a man who was made before me,
but that he might be made manifest in Israel,
therefore I came baptizing in water.
I saw the Holy Spirit descending as a dove from heaven,
and it remained and rested upon him.
behold: He had already come, and was not recognized; but now he is shown.
Behold, this is he whom the Patriarchs desired,
Behold the innocent one among sinners,
the just one among the reprobate,
the holy one among the impious;
in whom no sin could be found.
See how, like a lamb, he is led to the sacrifice and does not open his mouth.
He comes a second time to John, that no one might suspect
that he came as the others did — to confess his sins, or to be washed in penance.
No: he comes to give John occasion to speak of him,
and John corrects the suspicion, calling him the Lamb and Redeemer,
free from every sin that was in the whole world.
consider: He is called the Lamb of God — that is, sent from God, to be offered as a most acceptable victim.
He takes away the sin of the world, for to take away sins belongs to God alone.
This is the cause for which he came:
that, taking upon himself the sins of a perishing world,
he might abolish in himself — who could not be conquered — the stain of sin and the death of all.
As the paschal lamb was without blemish, so Christ was without sin;
and as Israel was freed by its blood from the slavery of Egypt,
so by his Passion we are freed from the slavery of the devil.
Theophylact says: he did not say 'who shall take away,' but 'who takes away,'
as though he is ever doing this.
For he did not take away sin only when he suffered,
but from that time even until now he takes it away — though not always crucified.
One offering he made for sins, but through it he is forever cleansing.
He washes us in his blood — not only on the cross, not only in baptism,
but daily, when the memory of his blessed Passion is renewed at the altar,
when bread and wine are made the sacrament of his flesh and blood,
when we are filled with his most holy Body and Blood.
And consider this image: as long as the spirit stays shut within the body,
though the body be cast into the water, it is tossed by the waves but never drowns;
but if the water seeps in through any part, the spirit goes out, and the body sinks and perishes.
So those who are in the waters of this world — of riches and fleeting delights —
if they keep the Holy Spirit shut within by the love of God and neighbor,
though shaken by the floods of temptation and trouble, are never drowned.
But where sin seeps in, the Spirit withdraws.
When we are angry, when we slander, when we cling to the things of the flesh,
know that the Holy Spirit does not remain in us.
prayer: among the sheep to be set at your right hand.
But first forgive me my sins and offenses,
that you may the better acknowledge me among your sheep.
Lamb of God, have mercy on me.
Lamb of God, have mercy on me.
Lamb of God, give me peace.

latinJewel.latin: Ecce innocens inter peccatores, justus inter reprobos, pius inter impios; in quo nullum peccatum potuit inveniri.
latinJewel.english: Behold the innocent one among sinners, the just one among the reprobate, the holy one among the impious; in whom no sin could be found.
